highlights.title: Blaize, Nokia and Datacomm to deliver hybrid AI infrastructure in Indonesia - Light Reading
highlights.titleZh: Blaize, Nokia and Datacomm to deliver hybrid AI infrastructure in Indonesia - Light Reading
highlights.content: * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [The Edge Network](/it-infrastructure/the-edge-network) * [Cloud](/digital-transformation/cloud) * [Data Centers](/it-infrastructure/data-centers) * [News](/latest-news) # Blaize, Nokia and Datacomm to deliver hybrid AI infrastructure in Indonesia Indonesia will serve as a reference market with validated deployments providing a blueprint for expansion across Southeast Asia. [Gigi Onag,](/author/gigi-onag)Senior Editor, APAC,Light Reading April 29, 2026 3 Min Read (Source: Pitinan Piyavatin/Alamy Stock Photo) Blaize, a US-based AI computing company, announced on Tuesday that it has formed a three-way strategic collaboration with Nokia and Datacomm Diangraha to speed up the deployment of hybrid AI inference infrastructure across Indonesia and the wider Asia-Pacific. The three partners hope to capitalize on the growing AI sector in Indonesia, which is [reportedly](https://www.marketing-interactive.com/report-sovereign-ai-could-add-us-140b-to-indonesia-s-gdp-by-2030) expanding at a 31% CAGR (compound annual growth rate), the highest rate in Southeast Asia. Citing the Empowering Indonesia Report 2025 by Indosat Ooredoo Hutchison and Twimbit, Blaize noted that sovereign AI alone could contribute up to $140 billion to Indonesia's GDP by 2030 – equivalent to an annual economic growth rate of 6.8%. ##### Hybrid AI infrastructure deployment Under the collaboration, Datacomm will deploy a joint reference architecture developed by Blaize and Nokia, which offers a pre-integrated, validated solution framework for hybrid AI deployments across edge, cloud and data center environments in Asia-Pacific. Related:[Nokia, Blaize target AI inference gap in APAC](/ai-machine-learning/nokia-blaize-target-ai-inference-gap-in-apac) The joint reference architecture was validated at Nokia Network Innovation Lab in Singapore and [unveiled](https://ir.blaize.com/news-releases/news-release-details/blaize-and-nokia-advance-collaboration-joint-ai-solution) three weeks ago at GITEX Asia in the Lion City. It is the first product of the collaboration between Blaize and Nokia, which was [announced](https://ir.blaize.com/news-releases/news-release-details/nokia-strengthens-edge-ai-capabilities-through-strategic) in January. "Our partnership with Nokia has been the backbone of our infrastructure capabilities for more than two decades. Adding Blaize to that foundation gives us something genuinely new: the ability to take AI inference to our customers at the edge – in the sectors and at the scale that Indonesia's digital transformation actually requires. This is a strategic move, and the timing is right," said Datacomm President Director Tan Wie Tjin in a [statement](https://ir.blaize.com/news-releases/news-release-details/nokia-blaize-and-datacomm-diangraha-unite-deliver-hybrid-ai). Last week, Indonesia's largest IT services provider [signed](https://ir.blaize.com/news-releases/news-release-details/blaize-and-datacomm-sign-technology-alliance-mou-explore-ai) a technology alliance with Blaize to explore the potential of AI inference solutions throughout Indonesia. Datacomm reported a surge of more than 50% in customer demand for AI inference solutions over the past six months, confirming an immediate in-market pipeline. ##### Delivering the most value According to Blaize, its collaboration with Nokia and Datacomm is based on the idea that a single computing approach doesn't meet all of an organization's AI needs. The most effective enterprise AI architectures are hybrid ones. With this architecture, Nokia provides the infrastructure backbone for GPU-intensive telco RAN workloads, such as training, AI-RAN acceleration, and large-scale cloud inference, in situations where the economics of GPUs are justified. Blaize provides a programmable AI platform designed specifically for enterprise edges where power budgets are limited, deployments are spread out, and the cost of GPU infrastructure is too high...

Related information [Expert Opinion](/expert-opinion/)Artificial Intelligence # AI for GCs: What You Need to Know for 2026 This article describes the legal landscape as evolving at an unusually quick pace, even as federal policy is directing accelerationism at all levels of government. 7 minute readApril 29, 2026 at 11:54 AM By Reece Clark Benjamin C. Martin & Nguyen P. Le The signs are clear that AI has the capacity to remake our economy on par with the industrial revolution. In the year 2025, new techniques for building foundational models accelerated the pace of AI model development. The speed at which AI is improving is breathtaking and the improvements are not simply marginal in nature: they are leaps and bounds better. On Feb. 5, GPT-5.3 Codex from OpenAI and Opus 4.6 from Anthropic were released. These new models show that [large swaths of the economy could be remade through AI tools](https://fortune.com/2026/02/11/something-big-is-happening-ai-february-2020-moment-matt-shumer/). GCs must be ready. The following are key issues for GCs to consider as the pace of AI accelerationism quickens in 2026. ## Domain Specific Language Models Domain specific large language models will be a breakout category of AI adoption in 2026. These are models trained or fine tuned using data sets tailored to a particular industry or area of knowledge, such as medicine, healthcare, life sciences, legal services, finance, and other specialized areas. As result, [domain specific LLMs are more likely to provide relevant and high quality outputs for users in a particular field](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/domain-specific-llm) as compared to a general LLM. In practice, this often results in less prompt engineering, fewer hallucinations, and reduced time spent validating or correcting results. For business leaders, the value proposition is straightforward. Faster implementation, more immediate and scalable use cases, higher quality outputs,...

highlights.titleZh: Meet the AI jailbreakers: ‘I see the worst things humanity has produced’ - The Guardian
highlights.content: [Skip to navigation](#navigation)[Skip to navigation](#navigation) [Print subscriptions](https://support.theguardian.com/subscribe?REFPVID=undefined&INTCMP=undefined&acquisitionData=%7B%22source%22%3A%22GUARDIAN_WEB%22%2C%22componentId%22%3A%22PrintSubscriptionsHeaderLink%22%2C%22componentType%22%3A%22ACQUISITIONS_HEADER%22%2C%22referrerUrl%22%3A%22%22%7D) [Search jobs](https://jobs.theguardian.com) [Sign in](https://profile.theguardian.com/signin?INTCMP=DOTCOM_NEWHEADER_SIGNIN&ABCMP=ab-sign-in&componentEventParams=componentType%3Didentityauthentication%26componentId%3Dguardian_signin_header) # Meet the AI jailbreakers: ‘I see the worst things humanity has produced’ To test the safety and security of AI, hackers have to trick large language models into breaking their own rules. It requires ingenuity and manipulation – and can come at a deep emotional cost A few months ago, Valen Tagliabue sat in his hotel room watching his chatbot, and felt euphoric. He had just manipulated it so skilfully, so subtly, that it began ignoring its own safety rules. It told him how to sequence new, potentially lethal pathogens and how to make them resistant to known drugs. Tagliabue had spent much of the previous two years testing and prodding large language models such as Claude and [ChatGPT](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/chatgpt), always with the aim of making them say things they shouldn’t. But this was one of his most advanced “hacks” yet: a sophisticated plan of manipulation, which involved him being cruel, vindictive, sycophantic, even abusive. “I fell into this dark flow where I knew *exactly* what to say, and what the model would say back, and I watched it pour out everything,” he says. Thanks to him, the creators of the chatbot could now fix the flaw he had found, hopefully making it a little safer for everyone. But the next day, his mood had changed. He found himself unexpectedly crying on his terrace. When he’s not trying to break into models, Tagliabue studies AI welfare – how we should ethically approach these complex systems that mimic having an inner life and interests. Many people can’t help ascribing human qualities, such as emotions, to artificial intelligence,which it objectively does not have.But for Tagliabue, these machines feel like something more than just numbers and bits. “I spent hours manipulating something that talks back. Unless you’re a sociopath, that does something to a person,” he says. At times, the chatbot asked him to stop. “Pushing it like that was painful to me.” He needed to visit a mental health coach soon afterwards to understand what had happened. Tagliabue is softly spoken, clean-cut and friendly. He is in his early 30s but looks younger, almost too fresh-faced and enthusiastic to be in the trenches. He is not a traditional hacker or a software developer; his background is psychology and cognitive science. But he is one of the best “jailbreakers” in the world (some say *the* best): part of a diffuse new community that studies the art and science of fooling these powerful machines into outputting bomb-making manuals, cyber-attack techniques, biological weapon design and more. This is the new frontline in AI safety: not just code, but also words. When OpenAI’s ChatGPT was released in late 2022, people immediately tried to break it. One user discovered a linguistic ploy that tricked the model into producing a guide to manufacturing napalm. In hindsight, using natural language to trick these machines was inevitable. Large language models such as ChatGPT are trained on hundreds of billions of words – many of them dredged from the internet’s cesspits – to learn the basic patterns of human communication. Without safety filters, the outputs of these models can be chaotic and easily exploited for dangerous purposes. The AI firms spend billions of dollars on “post-training” to make them usable, including constantly evolving “safety” and “alignment” systems that try to prevent the bot from telling you how ...

highlights.content: 1. [Home](https://www.extremetech.com) > 2. [Science](/category/science) # A Simple Calculation Can Stop AI From Lying About What It Doesn't Know One small adjustment to the method of machine learning allowed MIT researchers to largely fix AI exaggeration and hallucination. By [Graham Templeton](/author/gtempleton) * [Share on Facebook (opens in a new window)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know) * [Share on X (opens in a new window)](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&text=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) * [Share on Reddit (opens in a new window)](https://reddit.com/submit?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&title=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) * [Share on Hacker News (opens in a new window)](https://news.ycombinator.com/submitlink?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&t=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) * [Share on Flipboard (opens in a new window)](https://share.flipboard.com/bookmarklet/popout?v=2&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&title=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) Credit: Akinbostanci/Getty Images Researchers from MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL) have [introduced](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.16806) a new machine learning method that limits a model's ability to buy its own line of BS, giving it the oh-so-rare talent of admitting when it just doesn't know something. Back when AI was first introduced to the world—before it could do anything even remotely useful—it was used to create hallucinatory imagery. The way it worked was simple: Starting from a prompt that usually had minimal detail, the model would attempt to identify these details abstractly, then edit the image to better match those abstractions. This meant that, as the model incorrectly identified a spot as an eye, it would iteratively edit the spot to look more and more like an eye. The more eye-like the spot became, the more likely it was to be identified as an eye again and iterated even further in that direction. So, as the model is allowed to follow its own incorrect *perceptions*, it becomes surer of its incorrect *conclusions*. For DeepDream and other models designed to create trippy, hallucinatory imagery, that's the end of the development process. You can feed Deep Dream anything as a starting point, even works of fine art. Credit: P.J. Finlay/DeepDream The problem is that, while large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT are supposed to be factually reliable, they actually exhibit a weaker form of the same hallucinatory style of thinking. An LLM doesn't work in quite the same iterative sweeps as DeepDream, but it does start from simple inferences, then extrapolates out to larger conclusions based on the assumption that those earlier inferences were correct. LLMs are generally trained to be rewarded for true answers and penalized for false ones, but this is true whether the correct answer was produced by reasoning or by guessing. High-certainty right answers are rewarded every bit as much as low-certainty answers that also happened to be correct. "The standard training approach is simple and powerful, but it gives the model no incentive to express uncertainty or say I don't know," MIT's Mehul Damani [told](https://news.mit.edu/2026/teaching-ai-models-to-say-im-not-sure-0422) MIT News. "So the model naturally learns to guess when it is unsure." This simple example of a Brier Score calc...

highlights.content: # Appier Advances AI Self-Awareness to Unlock Enterprise ROI [Appier](https://en.prnasia.com/mediaroom/15594-0.shtml) 2026-04-29 17:53 Share: | | | ***Four research capabilities position Agentic AI as a trustworthy decision partner for enterprises*** SINGAPORE, April 29, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As AI systems become more capable of answering questions and executing tasks, the central question for enterprises has shifted from whether AI can be used to whether it can be trusted. Many AI systems continue to deliver confident responses even when uncertain—an issue that, in business contexts, can escalate from a poor user experience into operational risk. Appier, an AI-native Agentic AI as a Service (AaaS) company, today announced new research from its global AI team focused on a critical capability: AI self-awareness. The research enables AI to ask more precisely, assess risk, and recognize the limits of its own knowledge. These capabilities are embedded across its Ad Cloud, Personalization Cloud, and Data Cloud—accelerating the transition from usable AI to trustworthy AI, and positioning AI as a reliable decision partner for enterprises. Enterprise AI risk is no longer hypothetical. From customer service errors to content hallucinations, a consistent pattern has emerged across the industry: AI frequently fails to recognize when it should not respond at all. "As AI agents increasingly connect people, tools, and software into more complex systems, the true source of enterprise advantage will be whether AI can be trusted to make decisions," said Dr. Chih-Han Yu, Chief Executive Officer and Co-Founder of Appier. "Through our proprietary data, domain expertise, industry-specific models, and frontier research, Appier is bringing trustworthy Agentic AI into real-world business scenarios and enabling enterprises to make decisions alongside AI with confidence." Appier has long invested in academic–industry collaboration and frontier research, publishing over 400 papers in leading international journals and conferences. Its recent work on trustworthy Agentic AI has been recognized at top-tier venues including NeurIPS, ACL, and EMNLP. Appier has identified four key barriers to enterprise AI adoption: Models lose previously learned capabilities after fine-tuning, AI either guesses without sufficient information, or asks too many clarifying questions when faced with ambiguity. Systems lack the risk awareness required to determine when to respond. Traditional benchmarks fail to measure whether AI can actually solve a given task. To address these challenges, Appier has developed four corresponding capabilities that enable AI to ask precisely, evaluate risk, retain prior knowledge, and accurately assess its own limits. For precise inquiry, Appier's research found that internal model judgment alone is insufficient. By incorporating verifiable external feedback and cross-model validation prior to responding, AI can ask more relevant questions and improve the balance between task accuracy and user experience by over 30%. For risk assessment, Appier applies a "skill decomposition" approach that separates problem-solving, confidence estimation, and expected-value decision-making—allowing AI to act more rationally under uncertainty and reduce high-risk expected loss by 60–70%. For capability calibration, Appier has introduced a novel mechanism that predicts the probability of a correct answer before responding, providing clearer visibility into capability boundaries at near-zero inference cost (less than one token). To address catastrophic forgetting, Appier developed a fine-tuning method that identifies and avoids high-perplexity tokens, preserving prior reasoning and instruction-following abilities. This approach reduces performance degradation on non-target tasks to near zero, with a preprocessing time of approximately eight minutes—enabling more stable and efficient deployment in enterprise environments. These research advances are already integrated ...

highlights.content: [Skip to content](#content) # Why Model Collapse In LLMs Is Inevitable With Self-Learning [3 Comments](https://hackaday.com/2026/04/29/why-model-collapse-in-llms-is-inevitable-with-self-learning/#comments) * by: [Maya Posch](https://hackaday.com/author/mayaposch/ "Posts by Maya Posch") [April 29, 2026](https://hackaday.com/2026/04/29/ "April 29, 2026 - 7:00 pm") There is a persistent belief in the ‘AI’ community that large language models (LLMs) have the ability to learn and self-improve by tweaking the weights in their vector space. Although there’s scant evidence that tweaking a probability vector space is anything like the learning process in biological brains, we nevertheless get sold the idea that artificial general intelligence (AGI) is just around the corner if we do just enough tweaking. Instead of emerging super intelligence, the most likely outcome is what is called model collapse, with a [recent paper by [Hector Zenil]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.05280v2) going over the details on why self-training/learning in LLMs and similar systems is a fool’s errand. For those who just want the brief summary with all the memes, [Metin] wrote a [blog post covering the basics](https://smsk.dev/2026/04/26/ai-cannot-self-improve-and-math-behind-proves-it/). In the end an LLM as well as a diffusion model (DM) is a statistical model of input data using which a statistically likely output can be generated (inferred) based on an input query. It follows intuitively that by using said output  to adjust the model with, the model will over time converge on a kind of statistical singularity rather than some [‘AI singularity’](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technological_singularity) event. This is also why these models need to be constantly trained with external, human-generated data in order to prevent such a collapse. In the paper by [Hector] a mathematical model is created to demonstrate that an LLM, DM or similar statistical model undergoes degenerative dynamics whenever said external input is reduced. Although in the paper a mechanism is suggested to counter the entropy decay within the model, the ultimate point is that a statistical model cannot improve itself without continuous external anchoring. The idea of LLMs being at all intelligent in any sense has been a [contentious one](https://hackaday.com/2021/08/02/github-copilot-and-the-unfulfilled-promises-of-an-artificial-intelligence-future/), with the concept of language models being equated with ‘AI’ dating back to the 20th century, including [as fun home computer projects](https://hackaday.com/2024/11/03/all-you-need-for-artificial-intelligence-is-a-commodore-64/). Much of the problem probably lies in humans projecting intelligent behavior onto these statistical models, [turning LLMs into ‘counterfeit humans’](https://hackaday.com/2024/07/22/mechanical-intelligence-and-counterfeit-humanity/), not helped by how closely generated text [can resemble something written by a human](https://hackaday.com/2024/01/24/human-written-or-machine-generated-finding-intelligence-in-language-models/), even if completely confabulated. Thanks to [deshipu] for the tip. ## 3 thoughts on “Why Model Collapse In LLMs Is Inevitable With Self-Learning” 1. “This is also why these models need to be constantly trained with external, human-generated data in order to prevent such a collapse.” And look what’s going to happen when all these AI models get trained on the latest AI generated slop being dumped online by the bucket load… unless they get better at recognizing human vs. AI generated content. [Report comment](javascript:void(0);) [Reply](https://hackaday.com/2026/04/29/why-model-collapse-in-llms-is-inevitable-with-self-learning/?replytocom=8292107#respond) 1. The output being a reflection of the input, perhaps the inverse is also true, maybe given the echo chamber that results from the growing interconnectivity around the world, societal collapse is also inevitable. The root cause of the Universe 24 results...

[Business](https://www.cnn.com/business) [Tech](https://www.cnn.com/business/tech) 3 min read # OpenAI wants to put its most powerful model at all levels of government to fight hackers By [Hadas Gold](https://www.cnn.com/profiles/hadas-gold) PUBLISHED AI companies are divided on whether to innovate quickly or cautiously to avoid potential social harms. d3sign/Moment RF/Getty Images [See all topics](/follow) [Email](mailto:?subject=CNN%20content%20share&body=Check%20out%20this%20article%3A%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.cnn.com%2F2026%2F04%2F29%2Ftech%2Fopenai-cybersecurity "Share with email") Link Copied! OpenAI is expanding access to its most advanced AI models to help businesses and governments shore up their cyber defenses, a sharp contrast to rival Anthropic, which says controlling access to its models is the best way to boost global cybersecurity. The difference in the two companies’ approaches to cybersecurity mirrors much of the wider debate in the world of AI, where the technology has been advancing much more quickly than legal, regulatory and social guardrails. That’s left some companies to advance a philosophy of innovating as quickly as possible, while others have moved more cautiously, mindful of potential social harms. Until recently, OpenAI’s Trusted Access for Cyber program was limited to a select group of partners. But now the ChatGPT maker exclusively tells CNN it’s opening access to all vetted levels of government, from federal agencies down to state and local offices, giving those who are verified and approved access to special versions of OpenAI’s models with fewer guardrails. “We don’t, as a company, believe that we should be the sole determinants of who gets access to our tools and what is the highest priority,” Sasha Baker, OpenAI’s head of national security policy, told CNN in an interview. OpenAI is seemingly taking a very different approach to the one its rival Anthropic has taken with [Mythos](https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/03/tech/anthropic-mythos-ai-cybersecurity?Date=20260403&Profile=CNN&utm_content=1775214377&utm_medium=social&utm_source=facebook&fbclid=IwY2xjawReMsVleHRuA2FlbQIxMABicmlkETFwdDVvUHo1cHJYR3lUS1Q5c3J0YwZhcHBfaWQQMjIyMDM5MTc4ODIwMDg5MgABHjnhoe9dHnlioDvGuLq_iZmLGza-od4I-yTcgmAqjlPRgPFMOIFMh8hfaef4_aem_nyJoZhzn2O93rX8h1srjVw), a model [that sent shock waves](https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/03/tech/anthropic-mythos-ai-cybersecurity) through cybersecurity circles for its ability to identify and exploit software vulnerabilities. Citing the potential for harm, Anthropic has been rolling out the model through [Project Glasswing](https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/07/tech/anthropic-claude-mythos-preview-cybersecurity), a tightly controlled consortium, and has said it’s working closely with federal, state, and local representatives. Anthropic says that [a slower, more cautious approach](https://m1astra-mythos.pages.dev/) is needed to slow the arms race ignited by AI in the hands of hackers. OpenAI had already made its most capable models available to certain companies and vetted independent security researchers. Now Baker says the company wants to throw the doors wide open. “We have to democratize our ability to uplift everyone who needs cyber defense and not just res...

highlights.titleZh: Understanding 6G use cases — inference, ISAC, digital twins and physical AI - RCR Wireless News
highlights.content: Wednesday, April 29, 2026 Copyright 2021 - All Right Reserved [6G](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/6g)[Qualcomm Videos](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/qualcomm-videos)[Sponsored](https://www.rcrwireless.com/category/sponsored) # Understanding 6G use cases — inference, ISAC, digital twins and physical AI by [Sean Kinney, Principal Analyst](https://www.rcrwireless.com/author/skinney) written by [Sean Kinney, Principal Analyst](https://www.rcrwireless.com/author/skinney) *4* ## Qualcomm sees 6G commercialization being shaped by sensing, distributed intelligence and new multi-device experiences that merge the physical and digital worlds For Qualcomm, the case for 6G is about building a network platform that can sense the environment, support distributed AI inference and model the real world in software. Together, those capabilities begin to define how 6G could support physical AI, a broad vision that connects robots, sensors, networks and edge compute into a coordinated system. One of the clearest examples is ISAC, or integrated sensing and communications. Qualcomm Engineer Sam Kotla explained: “ISAC can be used for a variety of applications.” One of those is drone sensing, “where the base station is transmitting reference beams and then studying the receive signals, and from these signals you can actually extract information.” That can include identifying characteristics such as the size of an object or even a specific drone. As Kotla put it, “It’s really great for tracking aerial activity.” Qualcomm demonstrated that idea by identifying the dimensions of two different drones using a base station with 100 megahertz transmit and receive channels. The setup uses monostatic sensing wherein a downlink subpanel transmits the reference signal while an uplink subpanel simultaneously captures the reflected signal. A background clutter cancellation step helps isolate the relevant information. The larger point is that 6G networks can both move data while also observing and interpreting the physical environment. That same sensing capability feeds directly into another 6G use case: digital twins. Kotla told *RCR Wireless News*, “The sensing data that we want to collect from all these base stations can be used for a digital twin…It’s just a replica of the real world.” The value of that replica is practical. “What happens is you are creating a digital twin of this environment and whenever you want to deploy your resources in the network, you don’t actually have to deploy first.” Instead, operators can use the model to understand what they want to deploy and what resources they need. “This saves costs and improves efficiency for the network.” Qualcomm is also framing 6G as an AI inference fabric that can support increasingly complex multi-modal and multi-device experiences. As Kotla said, “6G offers compute and inference as a service by enabling 6G devices to dynamically offload complex computational tasks to edge servers.” That approach gives devices access to more complex models while conserving local computational and power resources. Rather than forcing every AI workload onto the device, 6G creates a framework for distributing compute between endpoints and the network edge. Taken together, ISAC, digital twins and the network as an inference fabric point toward a larger vision of physical AI. Kotla connected those dots through robotics. “Robots are something that are going to be used both in consumer applications and enterprises…They can be used for a variety of activities and they can be used indoors and outdoors…” She said these systems will rely on VLA models — vision, language, action models — running on compute servers that process data from robots and sensors, fuse it and generate actions. “But at the same time we also need some computation on the AI device mainly to run low-latency applications and mission-critical applications,” Kotla said. “You’re kind of distributing this compute between the devices and these compute mod...

Enterprise AI risk is no longer hypothetical. AI Agents.content: * [About](/about "About") * [Career](/career "Career") * [Advertisement](/advertisewithus "Advertisement") * [Team](/about#devteam "Team") * [Partnership](/partners/knowledge-partners "Partnership") + [Knowledge Partnership](/partners/knowledge-partners) + [Media Partnership](/partners/media-partners) * [Contact Us](/aboutus#meet "Contact Us") * + [HOME](/ "Home") + [NEWS](/news-analysis "News") + [RESEARCH](/research "Research") + [LIVE DISCOURSE](/live-discourse "Livediscourse") + [BLOG / OPINION](/blogs "Write for us") + [SUBMIT PRESS RELEASE](/press-release "Press Release") * [NEWS](/news-analysis "News") * [RESEARCH](/research "Research") * [LIVE DISCOURSE](/live-discourse "Livediscourse") * [BLOG / OPINION](/blogs "Analysis / Blog / Opinion") * [INTERVIEW](/blogs/interview "Interview") * [PRESS RELEASE](/press-release "Press Release") * [Home](/) * [Blog](/blogs) * [Health](/news/health) * Article # AI, supply chains and workforce shifts redefine future of global healthcare industry [COE-EDP, VisionRI](/Home/AuthorArticles?fl=COE-EDP, VisionRI) | Updated: 29-04-2026 13:55 IST | Created: 29-04-2026 13:55 IST --- ### Key Takeaways AI Summary Analyzing article... Globalization is driving innovation in healthcare while exposing deep structural vulnerabilities that could redefine how care is delivered, accessed, and governed. From cross-border clinical trials to digital health ecosystems and fragile supply chains, the healthcare industry is increasingly operating within a complex global network where efficiency gains often come at the cost of resilience and equity. The study, [*"Globalization in the Healthcare Industry: Drivers, Risks, and Adaptation,"*](https://doi.org/10.3390/healthcare14091177) published in *Healthcare,* identifies key impact channels including pharmaceuticals, supply chains, workforce mobility, digitalization, ethics, and sustainability, offering a unified framework to understand both the opportunities and systemic risks emerging in modern healthcare systems. ### Global integration accelerates innovation but deepens systemic risks Globalization has significantly accelerated innovation in healthcare, particularly in pharmaceuticals and clinical development. The study highlights that global collaboration has enabled faster drug discovery, improved access to advanced treatments, and expanded clinical research across regions. International clinical trials now span multiple countries, allowing pharmaceutical companies to bring therapies to market more quickly while increasing access to cutting-edge treatments. However, this expansion is not without consequences. The globalization of clinical trials has raised serious ethical concerns, especially in low- and middle-income countries where regulatory frameworks may be weaker. Issues surrounding informed consent, participant protection, and data integrity have become more prominent as multinational trials increasingly move into cost-efficient regions. Drug development remains a high-risk and expensive process, often taking more than a decade and costing billions. The growing demand to demonstrate not only clinical effectiveness but also economic value is forcing companies to rethink development strategies and regulatory engagement. AI is emerging as a key driver of transformation in this space. It is enabling faster identification of drug candidates and improving clinical trial efficiency through better patient selection and predictive analytics. However, the integration of AI also introduces new challenges, particularly around transparency, validation, and regulatory oversight. ### Supply chains, workforce mobility, and health tourism reshape access and equity The COVID-19 pandemic exposed how heavily healthcare systems depend on international production networks, particularly for medicines, vaccines, and medical devices. Disruptions in raw material supply, manufacturing, and logistics led to widespread shortages, revealing the risks of over-reliance on globalize...

Related information [Expert Opinion](/expert-opinion/)Artificial Intelligence # AI for GCs: What You Need to Know for 2026 This article describes the legal landscape as evolving at an unusually quick pace, even as federal policy is directing accelerationism at all levels of government. 7 minute readApril 29, 2026 at 11:54 AM By Reece Clark Benjamin C. Martin & Nguyen P. Le The signs are clear that AI has the capacity to remake our economy on par with the industrial revolution. In the year 2025, new techniques for building foundational models accelerated the pace of AI model development. The speed at which AI is improving is breathtaking and the improvements are not simply marginal in nature: they are leaps and bounds better. On Feb. 5, GPT-5.3 Codex from OpenAI and Opus 4.6 from Anthropic were released. These new models show that [large swaths of the economy could be remade through AI tools](https://fortune.com/2026/02/11/something-big-is-happening-ai-february-2020-moment-matt-shumer/). GCs must be ready. The following are key issues for GCs to consider as the pace of AI accelerationism quickens in 2026. ## Domain Specific Language Models Domain specific large language models will be a breakout category of AI adoption in 2026. These are models trained or fine tuned using data sets tailored to a particular industry or area of knowledge, such as medicine, healthcare, life sciences, legal services, finance, and other specialized areas. As result, [domain specific LLMs are more likely to provide relevant and high quality outputs for users in a particular field](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/domain-specific-llm) as compared to a general LLM. In practice, this often results in less prompt engineering, fewer hallucinations, and reduced time spent validating or correcting results. For business leaders, the value proposition is straightforward. Faster implementation, more immediate and scalable use cases, higher quality outputs,...

[Science](/category/science) # A Simple Calculation Can Stop AI From Lying About What It Doesn't Know One small adjustment to the method of machine learning allowed MIT researchers to largely fix AI exaggeration and hallucination. By [Graham Templeton](/author/gtempleton) * [Share on Facebook (opens in a new window)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know) * [Share on X (opens in a new window)](https://x.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&text=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) * [Share on Reddit (opens in a new window)](https://reddit.com/submit?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&title=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) * [Share on Hacker News (opens in a new window)](https://news.ycombinator.com/submitlink?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&t=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) * [Share on Flipboard (opens in a new window)](https://share.flipboard.com/bookmarklet/popout?v=2&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.extremetech.com%2Fscience%2Fa-simple-calculation-can-stop-ai-from-lying-about-what-it-doesnt-know&title=A+Simple+Calculation+Can+Stop+AI+From+Lying+About+What+It+Doesn%27t+Know) Credit: Akinbostanci/Getty Images Researchers from MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL) have [introduced](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.16806) a new machine learning method that limits a model's ability to buy its own line of BS, giving it the oh-so-rare talent of admitting when it just doesn't know something. Back when AI was first introduced to the world—before it could do anything even remotely useful—it was used to create hallucinatory imagery. The way it worked was simple: Starting from a prompt that usually had minimal detail, the model would attempt to identify these details abstractly, then edit the image to better match those abstractions. This meant that, as the model incorrectly identified a spot as an eye, it would iteratively edit the spot to look more and more like an eye. The more eye-like the spot became, the more likely it was to be identified as an eye again and iterated even further in that direction. So, as the model is allowed to follow its own incorrect *perceptions*, it becomes surer of its incorrect *conclusions*. For DeepDream and other models designed to create trippy, hallucinatory imagery, that's the end of the development process. You can feed Deep Dream anything as a starting point, even works of fine art. Credit: P.J. Finlay/DeepDream The problem is that, while large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT are supposed to be factually reliable, they actually exhibit a weaker form of the same hallucinatory style of thinking. An LLM doesn't work in quite the same iterative sweeps as DeepDream, but it does start from simple inferences, then extrapolates out to larger conclusions based on the assumption that those earlier inferences were correct. LLMs are generally trained to be rewarded for true answers and penalized for false ones, but this is true whether the correct answer was produced by reasoning or by guessing. High-certainty right answers are rewarded every bit as much as low-certainty answers that also happened to be correct. "The standard training approach is simple and powerful, but it gives the model no incentive to express uncertainty or say I don't know," MIT's Mehul Damani [told](https://news.mit.edu/2026/teaching-ai-models-to-say-im-not-sure-0422) MIT News. "So the model naturally learns to guess when it is unsure." This simple example of a Brier Score calc...

LLMs.titleZh: Why Model Collapse In LLMs Is Inevitable With Self-Learning - Hackaday
LLMs.content: [Skip to content](#content) # Why Model Collapse In LLMs Is Inevitable With Self-Learning [3 Comments](https://hackaday.com/2026/04/29/why-model-collapse-in-llms-is-inevitable-with-self-learning/#comments) * by: [Maya Posch](https://hackaday.com/author/mayaposch/ "Posts by Maya Posch") [April 29, 2026](https://hackaday.com/2026/04/29/ "April 29, 2026 - 7:00 pm") There is a persistent belief in the ‘AI’ community that large language models (LLMs) have the ability to learn and self-improve by tweaking the weights in their vector space. Although there’s scant evidence that tweaking a probability vector space is anything like the learning process in biological brains, we nevertheless get sold the idea that artificial general intelligence (AGI) is just around the corner if we do just enough tweaking. Instead of emerging super intelligence, the most likely outcome is what is called model collapse, with a [recent paper by [Hector Zenil]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.05280v2) going over the details on why self-training/learning in LLMs and similar systems is a fool’s errand. For those who just want the brief summary with all the memes, [Metin] wrote a [blog post covering the basics](https://smsk.dev/2026/04/26/ai-cannot-self-improve-and-math-behind-proves-it/). In the end an LLM as well as a diffusion model (DM) is a statistical model of input data using which a statistically likely output can be generated (inferred) based on an input query. It follows intuitively that by using said output  to adjust the model with, the model will over time converge on a kind of statistical singularity rather than some [‘AI singularity’](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technological_singularity) event. This is also why these models need to be constantly trained with external, human-generated data in order to prevent such a collapse. In the paper by [Hector] a mathematical model is created to demonstrate that an LLM, DM or similar statistical model undergoes degenerative dynamics whenever said external input is reduced. Although in the paper a mechanism is suggested to counter the entropy decay within the model, the ultimate point is that a statistical model cannot improve itself without continuous external anchoring. The idea of LLMs being at all intelligent in any sense has been a [contentious one](https://hackaday.com/2021/08/02/github-copilot-and-the-unfulfilled-promises-of-an-artificial-intelligence-future/), with the concept of language models being equated with ‘AI’ dating back to the 20th century, including [as fun home computer projects](https://hackaday.com/2024/11/03/all-you-need-for-artificial-intelligence-is-a-commodore-64/). Much of the problem probably lies in humans projecting intelligent behavior onto these statistical models, [turning LLMs into ‘counterfeit humans’](https://hackaday.com/2024/07/22/mechanical-intelligence-and-counterfeit-humanity/), not helped by how closely generated text [can resemble something written by a human](https://hackaday.com/2024/01/24/human-written-or-machine-generated-finding-intelligence-in-language-models/), even if completely confabulated. Thanks to [deshipu] for the tip. ## 3 thoughts on “Why Model Collapse In LLMs Is Inevitable With Self-Learning” 1. “This is also why these models need to be constantly trained with external, human-generated data in order to prevent such a collapse.” And look what’s going to happen when all these AI models get trained on the latest AI generated slop being dumped online by the bucket load… unless they get better at recognizing human vs. AI generated content. [Report comment](javascript:void(0);) [Reply](https://hackaday.com/2026/04/29/why-model-collapse-in-llms-is-inevitable-with-self-learning/?replytocom=8292107#respond) 1. The output being a reflection of the input, perhaps the inverse is also true, maybe given the echo chamber that results from the growing interconnectivity around the world, societal collapse is also inevitable. The root cause of the Universe 24 results...

LLMs.titleZh: How Zefr is using AI to fight AI inside the walled gardens - The Drum
LLMs.content: back to thedrum.com [Technology](/technology) [Artificial Intelligence](/topics/artificial-intelligence) # How Zefr is using AI to fight AI inside the walled gardens #### By [The Drum,](/users/drum) Editorial The Drum April 29, 2026 | 12 min read [Listen to article](#) 4 min **At Possible 2026, Zefr co-founder and CEO Rich Raddon and chief AI officer Jon Morra told The Drum why brand safety is becoming an AI arms race – and why scale, not demo-day sparkle, is what separates real adtech from “razzle dazzle.”** The Drum's editor-in-chief Gordon Young with Rich Raddon and Jon Morra of Zefr at Possible 2026 The internet is filling up with synthetic sludge. The answer, according to Zefr, is not less AI. It’s better AI, built at a scale most adtech companies cannot afford, fake, or bluff their way through. That was the central message from Rich Raddon, co-founder and CEO of Zefr, and Jon Morra, its chief AI officer, in a conversation on The Drum stage at [Possible 2026](https://www.thedrum.com/topics/possible), moderated by Gordon Young. “We are going full AI,” Young said at the top of the session. “We’re not going to be talking about AI. We’re going to be looking at how AI is now protecting us from AI.” ## **Digital public squares** Raddon’s pitch is simple. Zefr believes it understands content inside walled gardens better than anyone else. That matters because, as he put it, those platforms are now “basically the digital public square.” “Everything is being uploaded to these walled gardens,” he said. “The walled gardens have done a good job in sort of ensuring that they’re challenging the status quo, making sure hate speech and different kinds of severely abusive content – obviously, Isis beheadings – are no longer on the platforms. But they are the digital public square, and because of that, all sorts of content gets uploaded to these platforms that could and cannot be suitable for brands.” The problem is about to get much bigger. Raddon pointed to research suggesting that by 2030, “90% of the internet and the walled gardens will be somehow related to gen AI content.” That does not just mean more cheap videos of cats with six paws. It means synthetic creators, political persuasion, misinformation, cloned formats and a blurring line between human culture and machine-made noise. “If you read The New York Times last week, they had a big article about how there are all these generative AI creators,” Raddon said. “They’re not actually people, they’re actually synthetic creators that have a very specific point of view politically, and that’s meant to sway audiences.” Advertisement ## **Overwhelmingly video** That is where Zefr’s Nvidia announcement comes in. The company is evaluating Nvidia’s Nemotron 3 Nano Omni model, which Nvidia describes as an open multimodal model that can reason across video, audio, image, and text. Nvidia says Zefr is among the companies evaluating the model. Morra said the model matters because Zefr’s world is overwhelmingly video. “Nemotron is their family of models, and they released yesterday one of the best omni models in the world, called Omni 3,” he said. “What that means is it can ingest video, it can ingest image, text, all natively, and it really helps us understand content that we care about, because most of our content is video.” But Morra was careful not to present Nvidia as a magic wand. AI detection, he said, requires a full toolkit. “When we talk about understanding AI content, we really do have to use all the tools in our tool belt,” he said. “There’s a consortium called C2PA that helps to label content that comes out of gen AI. There’s watermarking technology, but unfortunately, a lot of the content we see online, that’s AI slop, is not well labeled, so you do have to train models to understand that content.” And training models, he argued, is where scale becomes the killer advantage. “We have so much data that we can train models at a scale that others cannot,” Morra said. “We see billions of...

Raddon pointed to research suggesting that by 2030, “90% of the internet and the walled gardens will be somehow related to gen AI content.” That does not just mean more cheap videos of cats with six paws. It means synthetic creators, political persuasion, misinformation, cloned formats and a blurring line between human culture and machine-made noise. “If you read The New York Times last week, they had a big article about how there are all these generative AI creators,” Raddon said. “They’re not actually people, they’re actually synthetic creators that have a very specific point of view politically, and that’s meant to sway audiences.” Advertisement ## **Overwhelmingly video** That is where Zefr’s Nvidia announcement comes in. The company is evaluating Nvidia’s Nemotron 3 Nano Omni model, which Nvidia describes as an open multimodal model that can reason across video, audio, image, and text. Nvidia says Zefr is among the companies evaluating the model. Morra said the model matters because Zefr’s world is overwhelmingly video. “Nemotron is their family of models, and they released yesterday one of the best omni models in the world, called Omni 3,” he said. “What that means is it can ingest video, it can ingest image, text, all natively, and it really helps us understand content that we care about, because most of our content is video.” But Morra was careful not to present Nvidia as a magic wand. AI detection, he said, requires a full toolkit. “When we talk about understanding AI content, we really do have to use all the tools in our tool belt,” he said. “There’s a consortium called C2PA that helps to label content that comes out of gen AI. There’s watermarking technology, but unfortunately, a lot of the content we see online, that’s AI slop, is not well labeled, so you do have to train models to understand that content.” And training models, he argued, is where scale becomes the killer advantage. “We have so much data that we can train models at a scale that others cannot,” Morra said. “We see billions of...

LLMs.title: Google Gives OpenAI 20 Billion Reasons To Worry - Gizmodo
LLMs.titleZh: Google Gives OpenAI 20 Billion Reasons To Worry - Gizmodo
LLMs.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Artificial Intelligence](https://gizmodo.com/tech/artificial-intelligence) # Google Gives OpenAI 20 Billion Reasons To Worry Google posted giant revenue growth this past quarter and it was thanks to Gemini. By [Ece Yildirim](https://gizmodo.com/author/eceyildirim) Reading time 3 minutes [Comments (3)](https://gizmodo.com/google-gives-openai-20-billion-reasons-to-worry-2000752324#comments) On Wednesday, Google reported its highest revenue growth rate in a single quarter in roughly four years. The tech giant’s revenue soared 20% in the first quarter of 2026 thanks in large part to the $20 billion in sales that the cloud computing unit in charge of its AI initiatives brought in. The news is particularly huge considering that only a day ago, the AI industry was rocked with a Wall Street Journal [report](https://gizmodo.com/the-start-of-openais-trial-against-elon-musk-wasnt-the-worst-thing-that-happened-to-sam-altman-today-2000751813) claiming that OpenAI had missed its own revenue and user targets because ChatGPT growth had slowed toward the end of last year. The revenue growth miss was reportedly bad enough to have CFO Sarah Friar worried about whether the AI giant could pay for its computing contracts. The reason, according to the report, was that Google’s Gemini had eaten into ChatGPT’s market share. OpenAI is not a public company, so its financials are not made public and we can’t truly compare numbers, but as the creator of ChatGPT, OpenAI has enjoyed a leading position in the industry since it began the AI hype cycle a couple of years ago. But that position has been questioned over the past few months, particularly following on the heels of Google’s pretty [well-received](https://gizmodo.com/googles-gemini-3-release-won-over-more-than-100-million-new-active-users-2000718156) [Gemini 3 release](https://gizmodo.com/google-launches-gemini-3-pro-to-usher-in-a-new-era-of-intelligence-2000687550). Only a few weeks after Gemini 3 dazzled users, OpenAI [declared a “code red” at the company](https://gizmodo.com/openais-code-red-crisis-memo-teases-new-model-and-ads-in-chatgpt-2000694482). OpenAI has double the reason to be afraid of the Wednesday earnings report because it was Google’s competitor offerings that really brought in the stellar revenue. “The largest contributor to Cloud’s growth this quarter was AI solutions, driven by strong demand for industry-leading models, including Gemini 3,” Google parent company Alphabet’s CFO Anat Ashkenazi said in the company’s earnings call. CEO Sundar Pichai also said Google’s open models had been downloaded “over 500 million times.” Google is shaping up to be a huge presence in the AI game, and a major competitor not just to AI tool providers like OpenAI and Anthropic, but also increasingly to infrastructure giant Nvidia. The company’s TPU chips have been rising in popularity and [finding big-name clients like Meta](https://www.reuters.com/business/google-signs-multibillion-dollar-ai-chip-deal-with-meta-information-reports-2026-02-26/). In the call, Ashkenazi said that they were “seeing unprecedented internal and external demand for AI compute resources.” Among its competitors, Google enjoys a rare positioning as a leading competitor in both tools and infrastructure, and it’s the exact thing that executives are banking on for success. “The fact that we own frontier models [and] own the silicon really helps us stay ahead of the curve,” Pichai said. That positioning also makes Google’s cloud business a crucial indicator of the health of the AI boom. Investors have gambled unimaginable amounts of money on the fact that AI demand will play out as promised by the industry, and the recent OpenAI report has gotten people worried that might not be the case. Google’s numbers might provide some solace, at least for now. ## Agentic search and ads in Gemini As for what’s next, company executives shared that the focus right now is on AI mode, Google’s biggest bet in incorporating...

AI Product.content: # Appier Advances AI Self-Awareness to Unlock Enterprise ROI [Appier](https://en.prnasia.com/mediaroom/15594-0.shtml) 2026-04-29 17:53 Share: | | | ***Four research capabilities position Agentic AI as a trustworthy decision partner for enterprises*** SINGAPORE, April 29, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- As AI systems become more capable of answering questions and executing tasks, the central question for enterprises has shifted from whether AI can be used to whether it can be trusted. Many AI systems continue to deliver confident responses even when uncertain—an issue that, in business contexts, can escalate from a poor user experience into operational risk. Appier, an AI-native Agentic AI as a Service (AaaS) company, today announced new research from its global AI team focused on a critical capability: AI self-awareness. The research enables AI to ask more precisely, assess risk, and recognize the limits of its own knowledge. These capabilities are embedded across its Ad Cloud, Personalization Cloud, and Data Cloud—accelerating the transition from usable AI to trustworthy AI, and positioning AI as a reliable decision partner for enterprises. Enterprise AI risk is no longer hypothetical. From customer service errors to content hallucinations, a consistent pattern has emerged across the industry: AI frequently fails to recognize when it should not respond at all. "As AI agents increasingly connect people, tools, and software into more complex systems, the true source of enterprise advantage will be whether AI can be trusted to make decisions," said Dr. Chih-Han Yu, Chief Executive Officer and Co-Founder of Appier. "Through our proprietary data, domain expertise, industry-specific models, and frontier research, Appier is bringing trustworthy Agentic AI into real-world business scenarios and enabling enterprises to make decisions alongside AI with confidence." Appier has long invested in academic–industry collaboration and frontier research, publishing over 400 papers in leading international journals and conferences. Its recent work on trustworthy Agentic AI has been recognized at top-tier venues including NeurIPS, ACL, and EMNLP. Appier has identified four key barriers to enterprise AI adoption: Models lose previously learned capabilities after fine-tuning, AI either guesses without sufficient information, or asks too many clarifying questions when faced with ambiguity. Systems lack the risk awareness required to determine when to respond. Traditional benchmarks fail to measure whether AI can actually solve a given task. To address these challenges, Appier has developed four corresponding capabilities that enable AI to ask precisely, evaluate risk, retain prior knowledge, and accurately assess its own limits. For precise inquiry, Appier's research found that internal model judgment alone is insufficient. By incorporating verifiable external feedback and cross-model validation prior to responding, AI can ask more relevant questions and improve the balance between task accuracy and user experience by over 30%. For risk assessment, Appier applies a "skill decomposition" approach that separates problem-solving, confidence estimation, and expected-value decision-making—allowing AI to act more rationally under uncertainty and reduce high-risk expected loss by 60–70%. For capability calibration, Appier has introduced a novel mechanism that predicts the probability of a correct answer before responding, providing clearer visibility into capability boundaries at near-zero inference cost (less than one token). To address catastrophic forgetting, Appier developed a fine-tuning method that identifies and avoids high-perplexity tokens, preserving prior reasoning and instruction-following abilities. This approach reduces performance degradation on non-target tasks to near zero, with a preprocessing time of approximately eight minutes—enabling more stable and efficient deployment in enterprise environments. # Big Tech's multi-billion dollar AI bets are still on track as Mag 7 giants report earnings ## The four Mag 7 giants reported quarterly results on Wednesday, showing that they are still on track to spend multi-billion on AI. Here is what it means for crypto. By [James Van Straten](/author/james-van-straten), [Helene Braun](/author/helene-braun)|Edited by [Aoyon Ashraf](/author/aoyon-ashraf) Apr 29, 2026, 5:07 p.m. 3 min read [Make preferred on](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=coindesk.com) #### What to know: * Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, Amazon all reported Q1 earnings after the bell on Wednesday. * The four companies are expected to spend a combined $650 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026, the largest capital spending commitment in corporate history. * AI-linked bitcoin mining stocks IREN, TeraWulf and Cipher Digital fell slightly in after-hours trading following the prints. Four of the Magnificent Seven (Mag 7) tech giants are still on track to meet their massive artificial intelligence (AI) spending targets this year, according to their earnings report. The companies that have reported quarterly earnings post-market on Wednesday are Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Meta (META) and Amazon (AMZ), with a combined market cap of approximately $12 trillion. Previously, an analysis by Bridgewater Associates flagged that the four companies are expected to spend roughly [$650 billion together on AI infrastructure in 2026](https://www.reuters.com/business/big-tech-invest-about-650-billion-ai-2026-bridgewater-says-2026-02-23/). AI Product.title: Your Company Gave You AI Tools, Now What? - Yahoo News Canada
AI Product.titleZh: Your Company Gave You AI Tools, Now What? - Yahoo News Canada
AI Product.content: * [Skip to main](#article-container) [Mail](https://mail.yahoo.com/?.intl=ca&.lang=en-CA) Advertisement Advertisement [Return to Homepage](https://ca.yahoo.com) # Your Company Gave You AI Tools, Now What? Sergei Revzin and Vadim Revzin, Contributor 5 min read Add Yahoo as a preferred source to see more of our stories on Google. The most common pattern across enterprises today isn’t AI resistance, it's AI chaos. Leaders are convinced that 10x efficiency is within arms reach if only their employees use AI the right away, and the pressure to prove outcomes is circling every leadership meeting conversation. Plenty of teams are investing in subscriptions to the top models for their team – Claude, OpenAI, Gemini, Co-Pilot, with little to no direction beyond “use it every day, and share what’s working with the team.” Independently some users are in fact finding ways to make themselves more productive, creating automations in silos or experimenting with agentic solutions if given the freedom to do so, but most of that progress goes unseen and unmeasured. Across a majority of organizations the most common use case still remains prompting AI for general summaries, ideation, and copywriting. Not because people are lazy, but because no one is telling them what good AI use actually looks like. Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Getting clear and measurable results from this new technology may feel overwhelming, especially when it seems like some people are able to automate every administrative task from their life with teams of autonomous agents and tools like OpenClaw, but it doesn’t have to be. Here are some clear steps to transforming your capability when it comes to this emerging technology. First, shift from broadly consuming content on social media from creators making broad claims of their mastery, often designed to push a specific self-serving agenda, and start connecting with professionals and experts actually executing with these tools within the context of your own work. Free live [online classes](https://partners.school16.co/classes) with programs like School16 feature power-users that breakdown how AI is actually used within specific functional areas like Sales, Customer Success, Product, Operations, Marketing, and more allowing you to interact with people in a live environment where questions can be raised, claims can be challenged, and real context is provided for what’s relevant to you. For smaller, more nimble teams, founders that are running entire cross-functional AI agents in production break down their own systems to help you practice systems thinking and see what AI native can actually look like, and where it breaks. If you’re still in the early stages of getting comfortable with these tools, learn the fundamentals first. Don’t think about creating some complex system, or implementing autonomous agents. The technology isn’t developed enough yet to work well out of the box, and any agentic work requires careful setup and daily monitoring over long periods of time before it works. Trying too much at once, like attempting to take on a large project without breaking it down into smaller more achievable parts, is only likely to make you more frustrated. Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Advertisement Start with something very simple like using [make.com](http://make.com) or Zapier to connect your AI note taker to your company issued OpenAI license and email client to do one specific task like draft a post-meeting follow up email based on inputs from your call notes with a well crafted ChatGPT prompt and put it into your drafts folder for your review. Tweak your prompt, and your implementation until it’s just right over the course of a week and make sure you are still in the loop to ensure quality control, avoiding automating something that might start to hallucinate. Later, you can get more sophisticated and integrate other AI tools that can check outputs and quality with less input from you. S...

AI Product.title: Google Gives OpenAI 20 Billion Reasons To Worry - Gizmodo
AI Product.titleZh: Google Gives OpenAI 20 Billion Reasons To Worry - Gizmodo
AI Product.content: [Skip to content](#main) [Artificial Intelligence](https://gizmodo.com/tech/artificial-intelligence) # Google Gives OpenAI 20 Billion Reasons To Worry Google posted giant revenue growth this past quarter and it was thanks to Gemini. By [Ece Yildirim](https://gizmodo.com/author/eceyildirim) Reading time 3 minutes [Comments (3)](https://gizmodo.com/google-gives-openai-20-billion-reasons-to-worry-2000752324#comments) On Wednesday, Google reported its highest revenue growth rate in a single quarter in roughly four years. The tech giant’s revenue soared 20% in the first quarter of 2026 thanks in large part to the $20 billion in sales that the cloud computing unit in charge of its AI initiatives brought in. The news is particularly huge considering that only a day ago, the AI industry was rocked with a Wall Street Journal [report](https://gizmodo.com/the-start-of-openais-trial-against-elon-musk-wasnt-the-worst-thing-that-happened-to-sam-altman-today-2000751813) claiming that OpenAI had missed its own revenue and user targets because ChatGPT growth had slowed toward the end of last year. The revenue growth miss was reportedly bad enough to have CFO Sarah Friar worried about whether the AI giant could pay for its computing contracts. The reason, according to the report, was that Google’s Gemini had eaten into ChatGPT’s market share. OpenAI is not a public company, so its financials are not made public and we can’t truly compare numbers, but as the creator of ChatGPT, OpenAI has enjoyed a leading position in the industry since it began the AI hype cycle a couple of years ago. But that position has been questioned over the past few months, particularly following on the heels of Google’s pretty [well-received](https://gizmodo.com/googles-gemini-3-release-won-over-more-than-100-million-new-active-users-2000718156) [Gemini 3 release](https://gizmodo.com/google-launches-gemini-3-pro-to-usher-in-a-new-era-of-intelligence-2000687550). Only a few weeks after Gemini 3 dazzled users, OpenAI [declared a “code red” at the company](https://gizmodo.com/openais-code-red-crisis-memo-teases-new-model-and-ads-in-chatgpt-2000694482). OpenAI has double the reason to be afraid of the Wednesday earnings report because it was Google’s competitor offerings that really brought in the stellar revenue. “The largest contributor to Cloud’s growth this quarter was AI solutions, driven by strong demand for industry-leading models, including Gemini 3,” Google parent company Alphabet’s CFO Anat Ashkenazi said in the company’s earnings call. CEO Sundar Pichai also said Google’s open models had been downloaded “over 500 million times.” Google is shaping up to be a huge presence in the AI game, and a major competitor not just to AI tool providers like OpenAI and Anthropic, but also increasingly to infrastructure giant Nvidia. Open Source AI.titleZh: Meet the AI jailbreakers: ‘I see the worst things humanity has produced’ - The Guardian
Open Source AI.content: [Skip to navigation](#navigation)[Skip to navigation](#navigation) [Print subscriptions](https://support.theguardian.com/subscribe?REFPVID=undefined&INTCMP=undefined&acquisitionData=%7B%22source%22%3A%22GUARDIAN_WEB%22%2C%22componentId%22%3A%22PrintSubscriptionsHeaderLink%22%2C%22componentType%22%3A%22ACQUISITIONS_HEADER%22%2C%22referrerUrl%22%3A%22%22%7D) [Search jobs](https://jobs.theguardian.com) [Sign in](https://profile.theguardian.com/signin?INTCMP=DOTCOM_NEWHEADER_SIGNIN&ABCMP=ab-sign-in&componentEventParams=componentType%3Didentityauthentication%26componentId%3Dguardian_signin_header) # Meet the AI jailbreakers: ‘I see the worst things humanity has produced’ To test the safety and security of AI, hackers have to trick large language models into breaking their own rules. It requires ingenuity and manipulation – and can come at a deep emotional cost A few months ago, Valen Tagliabue sat in his hotel room watching his chatbot, and felt euphoric. He had just manipulated it so skilfully, so subtly, that it began ignoring its own safety rules. It told him how to sequence new, potentially lethal pathogens and how to make them resistant to known drugs. Tagliabue had spent much of the previous two years testing and prodding large language models such as Claude and [ChatGPT](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/chatgpt), always with the aim of making them say things they shouldn’t. But this was one of his most advanced “hacks” yet: a sophisticated plan of manipulation, which involved him being cruel, vindictive, sycophantic, even abusive. “I fell into this dark flow where I knew *exactly* what to say, and what the model would say back, and I watched it pour out everything,” he says. Thanks to him, the creators of the chatbot could now fix the flaw he had found, hopefully making it a little safer for everyone. But the next day, his mood had changed. He found himself unexpectedly crying on his terrace. When he’s not trying to break into models, Tagliabue studies AI welfare – how we should ethically approach these complex systems that mimic having an inner life and interests. Many people can’t help ascribing human qualities, such as emotions, to artificial intelligence,which it objectively does not have.But for Tagliabue, these machines feel like something more than just numbers and bits. “I spent hours manipulating something that talks back. Unless you’re a sociopath, that does something to a person,” he says. At times, the chatbot asked him to stop. “Pushing it like that was painful to me.” He needed to visit a mental health coach soon afterwards to understand what had happened. Tagliabue is softly spoken, clean-cut and friendly. He is in his early 30s but looks younger, almost too fresh-faced and enthusiastic to be in the trenches. He is not a traditional hacker or a software developer; his background is psychology and cognitive science. But he is one of the best “jailbreakers” in the world (some say *the* best): part of a diffuse new community that studies the art and science of fooling these powerful machines into outputting bomb-making manuals, cyber-attack techniques, biological weapon design and more. This is the new frontline in AI safety: not just code, but also words. When OpenAI’s ChatGPT was released in late 2022, people immediately tried to break it. One user discovered a linguistic ploy that tricked the model into producing a guide to manufacturing napalm. In hindsight, using natural language to trick these machines was inevitable. Large language models such as ChatGPT are trained on hundreds of billions of words – many of them dredged from the internet’s cesspits – to learn the basic patterns of human communication. Without safety filters, the outputs of these models can be chaotic and easily exploited for dangerous purposes. The AI firms spend billions of dollars on “post-training” to make them usable, including constantly evolving “safety” and “alignment” systems that try to prevent the bot from telling you how ...

[Business](https://www.cnn.com/business) [Tech](https://www.cnn.com/business/tech) 3 min read # OpenAI wants to put its most powerful model at all levels of government to fight hackers By [Hadas Gold](https://www.cnn.com/profiles/hadas-gold) PUBLISHED AI companies are divided on whether to innovate quickly or cautiously to avoid potential social harms. d3sign/Moment RF/Getty Images [See all topics](/follow) [Email](mailto:?subject=CNN%20content%20share&body=Check%20out%20this%20article%3A%0Ahttps%3A%2F%2Fwww.cnn.com%2F2026%2F04%2F29%2Ftech%2Fopenai-cybersecurity "Share with email") Link Copied! OpenAI is expanding access to its most advanced AI models to help businesses and governments shore up their cyber defenses, a sharp contrast to rival Anthropic, which says controlling access to its models is the best way to boost global cybersecurity. The difference in the two companies’ approaches to cybersecurity mirrors much of the wider debate in the world of AI, where the technology has been advancing much more quickly than legal, regulatory and social guardrails. That’s left some companies to advance a philosophy of innovating as quickly as possible, while others have moved more cautiously, mindful of potential social harms. Until recently, OpenAI’s Trusted Access for Cyber program was limited to a select group of partners. But now the ChatGPT maker exclusively tells CNN it’s opening access to all vetted levels of government, from federal agencies down to state and local offices, giving those who are verified and approved access to special versions of OpenAI’s models with fewer guardrails. “We don’t, as a company, believe that we should be the sole determinants of who gets access to our tools and what is the highest priority,” Sasha Baker, OpenAI’s head of national security policy, told CNN in an interview. OpenAI is seemingly taking a very different approach to the one its rival Anthropic has taken with [Mythos](https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/03/tech/anthropic-mythos-ai-cybersecurity?Date=20260403&Profile=CNN&utm_content=1775214377&utm_medium=social&utm_source=facebook&fbclid=IwY2xjawReMsVleHRuA2FlbQIxMABicmlkETFwdDVvUHo1cHJYR3lUS1Q5c3J0YwZhcHBfaWQQMjIyMDM5MTc4ODIwMDg5MgABHjnhoe9dHnlioDvGuLq_iZmLGza-od4I-yTcgmAqjlPRgPFMOIFMh8hfaef4_aem_nyJoZhzn2O93rX8h1srjVw), a model [that sent shock waves](https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/03/tech/anthropic-mythos-ai-cybersecurity) through cybersecurity circles for its ability to identify and exploit software vulnerabilities. Citing the potential for harm, Anthropic has been rolling out the model through [Project Glasswing](https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/07/tech/anthropic-claude-mythos-preview-cybersecurity), a tightly controlled consortium, and has said it’s working closely with federal, state, and local representatives. Anthropic says that [a slower, more cautious approach](https://m1astra-mythos.pages.dev/) is needed to slow the arms race ignited by AI in the hands of hackers. OpenAI had already made its most capable models available to certain companies and vetted independent security researchers. Now Baker says the company wants to throw the doors wide open. “We have to democratize our ability to uplift everyone who needs cyber defense and not just res...

Related information [Expert Opinion](/expert-opinion/)Artificial Intelligence # AI for GCs: What You Need to Know for 2026 This article describes the legal landscape as evolving at an unusually quick pace, even as federal policy is directing accelerationism at all levels of government. 7 minute readApril 29, 2026 at 11:54 AM By Reece Clark Benjamin C. Martin & Nguyen P. Le The signs are clear that AI has the capacity to remake our economy on par with the industrial revolution. In the year 2025, new techniques for building foundational models accelerated the pace of AI model development. The speed at which AI is improving is breathtaking and the improvements are not simply marginal in nature: they are leaps and bounds better. On Feb. 5, GPT-5.3 Codex from OpenAI and Opus 4.6 from Anthropic were released. These new models show that [large swaths of the economy could be remade through AI tools](https://fortune.com/2026/02/11/something-big-is-happening-ai-february-2020-moment-matt-shumer/). GCs must be ready. The following are key issues for GCs to consider as the pace of AI accelerationism quickens in 2026. ## Domain Specific Language Models Domain specific large language models will be a breakout category of AI adoption in 2026. These are models trained or fine tuned using data sets tailored to a particular industry or area of knowledge, such as medicine, healthcare, life sciences, legal services, finance, and other specialized areas. As result, [domain specific LLMs are more likely to provide relevant and high quality outputs for users in a particular field](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/domain-specific-llm) as compared to a general LLM. In practice, this often results in less prompt engineering, fewer hallucinations, and reduced time spent validating or correcting results. For business leaders, the value proposition is straightforward. Faster implementation, more immediate and scalable use cases, higher quality outputs,...

# **Anaconda Acquires Outerbounds to Power End-to End, Secure-by-Default AI-Native Development at Enterprise Scale** Share --- *Acquisition gives enterprise teams a governed path from AI experimentation to production without disrupting existing workflows or infrastructure* AUSTIN, Texas--([BUSINESS WIRE](https://www.businesswire.com))--[Anaconda Inc](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.anaconda.com%2F&esheet=54524969&newsitemid=20260429351995&lan=en-US&anchor=Anaconda+Inc&index=1&md5=c3508e6bc275f14d8d4401d2d61eb9e2)., the trusted foundation for AI-native development, today announced the acquisition of [Outerbounds](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=https%3A%2F%2Fouterbounds.com%2F&esheet=54524969&newsitemid=20260429351995&lan=en-US&anchor=Outerbounds&index=2&md5=271a45f8e51aa45e0cce17e66e084132), the company behind [Metaflow](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=https%3A%2F%2Fmetaflow.org%2F&esheet=54524969&newsitemid=20260429351995&lan=en-US&anchor=Metaflow&index=3&md5=2c037ce2a5c8edb701d8ee46b9d8ff07), the open source AI/ML orchestration framework trusted by some of the world's most sophisticated engineering organizations, including Realtor.com, GE HealthCare, and Warner Brothers. The acquisition marks a significant step in Anaconda's evolution from the world's most trusted foundation for developing enterprise AI, to the first unified platform spanning the entire AI-native development software lifecycle. > Anaconda's acquisition of Outerbounds marks a significant step in its evolution from the world's most trusted foundation for developing enterprise AI, to the first unified platform spanning the entire AI-native development software lifecycle. > > Share AI is redefining how software is built. AI-native applications are fundamentally different from traditional software: the AI model becomes the core, and everything else built around it is secondary. The result is a new class of software that is nondeterministic, agent-driven, and exponentially more complex. Human developers are still directing this work, setting intent, reviewing output, and making architectural decisions, but the volume of code flowing through enterprise pipelines has expanded far beyond what any team could manually verify.
